artifacts.content:
From: Michael Thompson <mthompson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: Sarah Jensen <sjensen@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Cc: Mark Ellingson <mellingson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Date: 2025-02-18 08:47:12 -0600
Subject: Council Packet Item – Snowplow Route Liability Memo

Sarah,

Following up on our conversation after last week’s Public Works Committee meeting, I’ve drafted the short narrative you asked for regarding the January 2025 storm and the ongoing issues with Unit #24. I tried to keep it high-level but honest about the operational and liability concerns.

One piece I included—which I know is a little personal, but it’s relevant—is the incident with my son Evan the morning of January 9. He called me from the Brainerd High School parking lot around 7:40 a.m. to say he’d nearly slid into the ditch on Highland Scenic Drive because that segment still hadn’t been plowed; Unit #24 was down again with another electrical failure. I also note that I had briefly considered reassigning Unit #24 to lighter-duty work near the high school and moving a newer truck to cover the longer hospital and east-side routes, but I ruled that out for now because, if I formally document that I knew my own kid was having trouble getting to school safely on those routes and didn’t push for the most reliable equipment there, it could expose the City—and me personally—to liability if there’s a future accident.

I want to be upfront in the cover memo that we still don’t have firm cost estimates or a clear preferred option from staff at this point, but I’m putting it on the agenda anyway so we can at least start talking about it. For the online agenda management system, where it asks for “estimated time needed,” I’d suggest you plug in 15 minutes for this discussion. Realistically, it could go longer if council members start sharing their own snowstorm stories, but 15 minutes seems like a reasonable placeholder.

If you’d like, I can stop by your office later this afternoon to walk through the draft—should only take about 10–15 minutes. Otherwise, I’m fine with you editing for tone and length as needed before it goes to the City Administrator.

Thanks,

Michael

Michael Robert Thompson
Public Works Director
City of Brainerd Public Works Department
(218) 555-0174

metadata.subject: Council Packet Item – Snowplow Route Liability Memo

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Thompson <mthompson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: Sarah Lindgren <slindgren@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Date: January 27, 2025 8:42 AM
Subject: Rough estimate for ICMA ethics inquiry form

Sarah,

I finally pulled up the ICMA ethics inquiry portal you mentioned. One of the fields asks for “submission details – estimated time needed,” and I’m trying to give them something realistic so they don’t schedule me into a slot I can’t make.

If we’re talking about just walking through the fleet replacement situation and the fallout from the last couple of storms, I’m guessing I’d need around **40–45 minutes**. That’s enough time to explain the history with Unit #24, what happened during the December 23–24, 2024 storm when I left Natalie’s Christmas choir concert early at Brainerd High School, and how the 4‑hour delay clearing Washington Street led to angry calls to my personal cell and that heated voicemail from Councilmember Peterson on Christmas Eve.

I also want time to touch on the January 10, 2025 storm and the notice of claim from attorney Mark Jensen at Jensen & Holt that specifically calls out my name and questions whether I ignored prior maintenance recommendations on Unit #24. That’s not a two‑sentence explanation.

Do you think I should round the estimate up to **an hour** so there’s room for questions, or stick with something like **30–40 minutes** so it doesn’t look like I’m over‑dramatizing it? I move that the Council approve this contract tonight even though we haven’t had time to get all the competitive quotes required under our normal purchasing policy, so we don’t lose the contractor’s open slot – that line from last week’s agenda is still rattling around in my head as a possible perception issue.

If you have a minute today, let me know what sounds reasonable.

– Michael

metadata.subject: Rough estimate for ICMA ethics inquiry form

artifacts.content:
Title: Drafting ICMA Inquiry – Time Block
Date: January 28, 2025
Time: 3:00 PM – 3:45 PM
Location: Michael’s Office – Brainerd City Hall
Attendees: Michael Thompson

Description:

Hold time to draft responses for the ICMA online ethics inquiry form regarding snowplow fleet management.

Goals for this block:
- Outline narrative of Unit #24 history and major incidents (especially the December 23–24, 2024 storm and the January 10, 2025 storm).
- Prepare concise “Summary of Issue” text for the web form.
- Decide on a realistic entry for the “submission details > estimated time needed” field.

Working assumptions:
- Target a **30–35 minute** conversation window when they follow up, not a quick check‑in. There’s no way to cover the December 23–24 event – leaving Natalie’s Christmas choir concert early, the 4‑hour delay on Washington Street, angry resident calls, and Councilmember Peterson’s voicemail – in less than **half an hour**.
- Also need time to mention the January 10 storm fallout and the notice of claim from attorney Mark Jensen at Jensen & Holt regarding the Oak Street crash near 8th Avenue NE.

Notes:
- Consider whether to mention that we briefly explored reassigning Dave Larson from the east‑side tandem route but pulled back because of the reduced schedule and work restrictions his orthopedic surgeon, Dr. Michael Jensen, documented after Dave’s November 2024 back surgery at St. Joseph’s Medical Center.
- Before submitting, re‑read the section on estimated durations in the ICMA guidance; they suggested planning for at least **20–30 minutes** for complex issues.

Reminder: Bring fleet maintenance logs and relevant council packet excerpts to reference while drafting.

From: Sarah Lindgren <slindgren@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: Michael Thompson <mthompson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Date: January 29, 2025 10:11 AM
Subject: Re: Time estimate for ethics consult form

Michael,

Circling back on your question about the “submission details > estimated time needed” field.

Given everything you’re trying to cover, I wouldn’t lowball it. When I did my ICMA consult about procurement procedures last year, they suggested I block out **about 30 minutes** on my end and told me they typically schedule **30–45 minutes** for anything involving potential liability, council dynamics, and personnel constraints.

Your situation is more layered than mine was. You’ve got:
- The December 23–24, 2024 storm where you left Natalie’s Christmas choir concert early and ended up with that 4‑hour delay on Washington Street, angry calls to your personal cell, and Peterson’s voicemail.
- The January 10, 2025 storm and the notice of claim from attorney Mark Jensen at Jensen & Holt over the Oak Street crash.
- The fact that one alternative – shifting routes so Dave Larson isn’t stuck with the east‑side tandem – is constrained by his reduced schedule and the 6‑hour shift / 20‑pound lifting limits after his November 2024 back surgery at St. Joseph’s.

All of that takes time to unpack. I’d put **35–40 minutes** as your estimate. That doesn’t lock you into an exact length, but it signals that this isn’t a quick **5‑ or 10‑minute** question.

You can always say in the comments that you’re fine if they schedule up to **an hour**, but I wouldn’t type that in the formal “estimated time needed” box.

– Sarah

metadata.subject: Re: Time estimate for ethics consult form

artifacts.content:
Title: Call with ICMA Ethics Advisor – Fleet / Council Issues
Date: February 4, 2025
Time: 1:00 PM – 1:40 PM
Location: Michael’s Office – Door Closed
Attendees: Michael Thompson; ICMA Ethics Advisor (TBD)

Description:

Phone consult arranged through ICMA ethics inquiry portal. Michael submitted online form with background on:
- Ongoing reliability problems with Unit #24 and impacts on snow operations.
- December 23–24, 2024 storm (left Natalie’s Brainerd High School Christmas choir concert early; 4‑hour delay on Washington Street; angry resident calls; voicemail from Councilmember Peterson alleging mismanagement).
- January 10, 2025 storm and subsequent notice of claim from attorney Mark Jensen at Jensen & Holt regarding the Oak Street crash near 8th Avenue NE.
- Constraints on reassigning Dave Larson due to his reduced schedule and medical restrictions (post‑surgery limits on shift length and lifting) after November 2024 back surgery at St. Joseph’s Medical Center.

The ICMA scheduler asked Michael to provide an entry for “submission details – estimated time needed.” Michael indicated **approximately 35 minutes** based on guidance that complex matters usually require **at least 25–30 minutes** of discussion, with extra time reserved if needed.

Agenda for call:
1. Brief recap (target **5–7 minutes**).
2. Discussion of council expectations and political pressures (target **10–15 minutes**).
3. Ethics considerations around procurement timing and messaging to Council, including how to handle comments like “I move that the Council approve this contract tonight even though we haven’t had time to get all the competitive quotes required under our normal purchasing policy, so we don’t lose the contractor’s open slot.” (target **10–15 minutes**).
4. Next steps and documentation (target **5 minutes**).

Note: Have council packets, maintenance logs, and legal correspondence printed and ready.

metadata.title: Call with ICMA Ethics Advisor – Fleet / Council Issues

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Thompson <mthompson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: ICMA Ethics Center <ethics@icma.org>
Date: January 30, 2025 7:19 PM
Subject: Clarification on time estimate field – snowplow fleet inquiry

Hello,

I’m in the process of completing your online ethics inquiry form related to snowplow fleet management and council decision‑making, and I had a question about the “submission details > estimated time needed” field.

The situation involves multiple incidents with one truck (Unit #24) and a mix of operational, legal, and political dimensions. For example, during the December 23–24, 2024 storm, I left my daughter Natalie’s Christmas choir concert at Brainerd High School early to respond to yet another breakdown of Unit #24 on Washington Street. That contributed to a roughly **4‑hour delay** in clearing our main east–west arterial, which triggered angry calls to my personal cell and a heated voicemail from one councilmember accusing me of mismanaging the fleet.

In addition, after the January 10, 2025 storm we received a notice of claim from attorney Mark Jensen at Jensen & Holt regarding an Oak Street crash allegedly tied to delayed plowing while Unit #24 was down with an electrical failure. We also briefly explored reassigning routes so my senior operator, Dave Larson, wouldn’t have to cover the east‑side tandem, but his reduced schedule and restrictions after November 2024 back surgery at St. Joseph’s Medical Center (documented by his orthopedic surgeon, Dr. Michael Jensen) complicate that option.

Given this complexity, is it more appropriate for me to estimate **around 30 minutes** of discussion time, or should I plan on **closer to 45 minutes** so there’s space for follow‑up questions? I don’t want to understate the time required, but I also don’t want to over‑request.

Thank you for any guidance.

Sincerely,

Michael R. Thompson
Public Works Director
City of Brainerd, MN

metadata.subject: Clarification on time estimate field – snowplow fleet inquiry

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Thompson <mthompson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: City Administrator <cityadmin@ci.brainerd.mn.us>; Council Packet Prep <clerk@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Date: 2025-02-18 09:42
Subject: CIP Memo – Draft language and review timing

I’ve attached the working draft of the Unit #24 replacement memo for the March CIP packet. Before I finalize the “Submission Details” section, I need your guidance on how specific we want to be about staff time.

Right now I’ve penciled in an **estimated time needed** for my portion of the council presentation at **20–25 minutes**, including Q&A. That assumes about 10 minutes to walk through the summary of the issue and alternatives, another 5–10 for financial implications, and a short wrap‑up with the recommended motion. If you think the agenda is going to be tight, I can trim it closer to 10 minutes, but anything shorter than that will make it hard to cover the recent storm incidents.

In the “Summary of Issue” paragraph, I referenced the February 5–7, 2025 blizzard where the hydraulic failure left the northeast arterial route unplowed for nearly 6 hours. That’s the same night my 73‑year‑old mom, Carolyn Thompson, slipped on the uncleared section of East River Road near St. Joseph’s and ended up in the ER with a fractured wrist. It’s a concrete example of why this isn’t just about convenience.

To be honest, I’m bringing this forward now because a few residents have been emailing council members directly and I’m tired of dealing with the complaints in my inbox. We haven’t completed a full cost estimate yet, but I’m hoping council will at least give a general thumbs‑up so we can figure out the numbers later.

Please let me know by Thursday how much time I should plan for on the agenda so I can adjust the memo language accordingly.

metadata.subject: CIP Memo – Draft language and review timing

artifacts.content:
Title: Prep – Council memo “Submission Details”
Date: 2025-02-21 07:30–08:10
Location: Home office – Maple Ridge Drive
Attendees: Michael Thompson

Description:
Block of time for Michael to tighten up the “Submission Details” portion of the Unit #24 replacement memo before sending it to the City Administrator.

Agenda:
- 10 min – Rework “Summary of Issue” paragraph to highlight February 5–7, 2025 blizzard (northeast arterial unplowed ~6 hrs; Carolyn’s fall on East River Road and ER visit for fractured wrist).
- 10 min – Clarify “Alternatives / Options / Effects on Others / Comments” section. Note that when considering the alternatives, Michael is very conscious of the effects on residents because during the February 9–10, 2025 storm, Natalie’s school bus to Forestview was nearly 25 minutes late getting down Oak Street after Unit #24 broke down again. Laura called him at the Public Works garage on Industrial Park Road around 7:15 a.m. because Natalie was panicking about missing her 8:00 a.m. counseling appointment with Dr. Karen Ellison at Essentia Health–Brainerd Clinic.
- 10 min – Draft language on temporary loss of on‑street parking and increased noise; note expectation that some residents will be pretty upset at first, but in past projects those complaints have usually died down after a while.
- 10 min – Decide what to plug in for **estimated time needed for presentation** (currently thinking **about 30 minutes total** including questions, but may shorten).

Reminder: Email revised draft to clerk by 9:00 a.m.

From: Dave Larson <dlarson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: Michael Thompson <mthompson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Date: 2025-02-20 03:11
Subject: Re: How long do you need for the plow truck item?

Mike,

Saw your note about the council agenda while I’m on break out here by County Road 3. If they’re asking you to fill in that “estimated time needed” box again, don’t sell it too short.

From where I sit, you probably need **a solid half hour** to lay everything out, especially if they start peppering you with questions about why we can’t just keep patching the old rig. When you get into the story about your mom falling during that February 5–7, 2025 blizzard because the northeast arterial sat unplowed for those 6 hours, they usually stop thinking it’s just a budget line.

If it helps, you can mention that March 10–11, 2023 blizzard when we were running the overnight route on County Road 3. That’s when I told you my divorce from Karen was turning violent, and we had to swing by the Crow Wing County Sheriff’s Office at 2:30 a.m. in the middle of the route because she showed up at the Public Works garage threatening to report our truck as unsafe. It shows how much extra chaos there is when the equipment’s already on the edge.

Anyway, if they really push you, maybe you could say **25–30 minutes**, but I wouldn’t go less than that. They need to hear the whole picture.

Get some sleep when you can.

– Dave

metadata.subject: Re: How long do you need for the plow truck item?

artifacts.content:
Title: Council Meeting – Unit #24 Replacement Presentation
Date: 2025-03-03 18:30–20:00
Location: Brainerd City Hall – Council Chambers
Attendees: Michael Thompson, City Council, City Administrator, Finance Director

Description:
Regular council meeting. Michael to present staff recommendation on replacement of Unit #24 and respond to council questions.

Item notes:
- Staff report already in packet; “Submission Details” section includes narrative on recent storm events and community impacts.
- Michael to cover:
  • Summary of Issue: February 5–7, 2025 blizzard hydraulic failure, northeast arterial unplowed ~6 hours, and resulting incident where his mother Carolyn slipped on East River Road near St. Joseph’s and went to ER with fractured wrist.
  • Alternatives / Options / Effects on Others / Comments: Discussion of route reshuffling, delays to school buses, and specific example from February 9–10, 2025 storm when Natalie’s bus to Forestview was ~25 minutes late down Oak Street after Unit #24 broke again, causing her to nearly miss an 8:00 a.m. counseling appointment with Dr. Karen Ellison at Essentia Health–Brainerd Clinic.
  • Resident impacts: Acknowledge some residents will be upset about temporary loss of on‑street parking and increased noise, but note that in past projects complaints usually die down after a while.
  • Recommended Action / Motion: Motion to approve the purchase as described so Michael and senior operator Dave Larson can avoid another situation like the March 10–11, 2023 blizzard detour to the Crow Wing County Sheriff’s Office.

Estimated time needed on agenda: **approximately 25 minutes**, including questions.

From: Michael Thompson <mthompson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: Jennifer Bergman <jbergman@ci.brainerd.mn.us>; Council Packet <council@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Date: 2026-02-23 08:41
Subject: Tentative requested meeting date for Unit #24 replacement agenda item

Jennifer and Council,

I’m finalizing the agenda item basics form for the Unit #24 tandem plow replacement and need to plug in a requested meeting date so Clerk’s staff can queue the packet. Right now, I’m leaning toward asking that it be placed on the **April 6, 2026** regular Council agenda. That should give Finance enough time to verify the lease-purchase numbers and still keep us on track with the build slot the Northland Municipal Equipment rep is holding.

To explain the timing, I’m trying to get this truck ordered before I have to take intermittent FMLA time this summer to help my mom, Carolyn Thompson, who’s been undergoing chemotherapy at the St. Cloud Hospital cancer center since August 2025 and just had her oncologist, Dr. Patel, extend treatments through at least September 2026. If I wait, I’m worried I’ll be out of the office more frequently for her appointments and won’t be able to properly oversee the build spec review and pre-delivery inspection, given I already used 64 hours of sick and family leave between October and December 2025 for her earlier rounds.

If April 6 doesn’t work for some reason, another option would be the **March 2, 2026** work session, but that may be tight for getting the recommended action motion and supporting documentation polished.

Please let me know which date you prefer so I can lock it into the form this afternoon.

Thanks,
Michael
Public Works Director

metadata.subject: Tentative requested meeting date for Unit #24 replacement agenda item

artifacts.content:
Title: Draft requested meeting date – plow replacement agenda item
Date: 2026-02-27 10:00–11:00
Location: Michael’s Office – City Hall
Attendees: Michael Thompson; Jennifer Bergman (optional)

Description:

Block of time for Michael to complete the “agenda item basics” section for the Unit #24 replacement request before the internal deadline. Goal is to settle on a requested meeting date to list on the form and confirm that with Jennifer.

Working assumptions going into this:

- Primary target is the **April 20, 2026** regular Council meeting, which should line up with Finance’s second-quarter amendment discussion.
- Backup option is the **March 30, 2026** work session if Council prefers to talk through the alternatives before a formal vote.

Notes to self:

- One alternative would be to postpone this project a year or two, but that would give us more time to sort out some of the neighborhood pushback and avoid a fight right now. If that comes up, be ready to explain why delay is risky in terms of operator fatigue and liability.
- Update the summary of issue to reflect how repeated breakdowns of Unit #24 during major storms in January and February 2025 forced us into unsustainable overtime and staffing decisions, including having senior operator Dave Larson cover a double route on January 18, 2025 after already working a 12‑hour shift. Dave later told me at the Public Works garage on College Drive that this contributed to the stress that led him to start seeing Dr. Karen Ellis at the Brainerd Behavioral Health Clinic for anxiety and sleep issues.

Reminder: Once the date is picked, email City Clerk staff so they can pencil the item onto that agenda.

metadata.title: Draft requested meeting date – plow replacement agenda item

artifacts.content:
From: Jennifer Bergman <jbergman@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: Michael Thompson <mthompson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Date: 2026-02-28 15:12
Subject: RE: Requested Council date for plow purchase item

Michael,

I looked at the upcoming agendas with the Clerk and Mayor. Given how full March is with the frontage road hearing, I’d prefer we list your plow purchase item with a requested meeting date of **April 13, 2026** on the agenda item basics form. That keeps it in front of Council before construction season ramps up but gives us breathing room on the packet.

If you feel strongly that it needs to go sooner, the only other realistic landing spot is the **March 9, 2026** regular meeting, but I’m worried that would cram too many high‑dollar items into one night.

I know this project wasn’t included in the current CIP or budget discussions, but I’m asking council to consider it as an exception because it’s been on the back burner for years and I didn’t push it hard enough before. You can echo that sentiment in your staff memo if you think it helps frame the urgency.

Also, I appreciate your transparency about the January 12, 2024 snow event and what happened with Evan on Highway 25. The way you explained that the sheriff’s deputy later told you at the Crow Wing County Sheriff’s Office that the windrows and ice ruts weren’t up to our usual standard because we’d had two trucks down, including Unit #24, really underscores the risk. That’s the kind of context Council needs to hear.

Let me know by Monday morning which date you want me to relay to the Clerk.

Jennifer
City Administrator

metadata.subject: RE: Requested Council date for plow purchase item

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Thompson <mthompson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: City Clerk’s Office <clerk@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Cc: Jennifer Bergman <jbergman@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Date: 2026-03-01 09:06
Subject: Agenda item basics – placeholder requested meeting date

Hi all,

I’m working through the online agenda item basics form for the “Unit #24 Tandem Plow Replacement – Purchase Authorization” request. Until Jennifer and the Mayor give me a final go‑ahead, please use **May 4, 2026** as a placeholder for the requested meeting date on your internal tracking list.

I understand that once we settle on the actual date, I’ll need to update the form so that the recommended action motion and supporting documents are tied to the correct Council meeting. Right now, the draft motion I’m planning to include reads along the lines of: “Motion to approve the purchase in the amount not to exceed $312,750.00 so that I can demonstrate to the City Administrator and HR that I’ve addressed the ‘equipment reliability and route coverage’ concerns cited in my informal performance warning dated April 8, 2024, after the March 31, 2024 complaint from Councilmember Harris about unplowed sections of Maple Ridge Drive, including my own residence at 714 Maple Ridge Drive, during a late‑season snowfall.”

Separately, I’ll also be drafting a companion change‑order item for the storm sewer project. The motion there will likely read, “I move that the Council retroactively approve the work that Public Works already directed the contractor to start last week so that we can get the paperwork cleaned up.” That one will be for a different meeting date.

Thanks for penciling this in while we sort out the schedule.

Michael
Public Works Director

metadata.subject: Agenda item basics – placeholder requested meeting date

artifacts.content:
Title: Council agenda prep – confirm requested meeting date for Unit #24 item
Date: 2026-03-05 14:30–15:30
Location: Conference Room B, City Hall
Attendees: Michael Thompson; Finance Director; City Administrator; City Clerk

Description:

Working session to finalize which Council meeting date Michael should list as the requested meeting date on the agenda item basics form for the Unit #24 tandem plow replacement.

Tentative options to discuss:

- **March 23, 2026** regular Council meeting – fastest path, but packet would be tight.
- **April 27, 2026** regular Council meeting – more time for public communication about route impacts.

Agenda:

1. Review draft staff report language, including the explanation for why Michael is favoring the Northland Municipal Equipment quote over trying to rebid locally. He’ll recap that in April 2023, when the city bid the last tandem, a Brainerd‑based dealer informally told him after the bid opening at City Hall that if we didn’t “stop hiding behind the state contract” and give him a shot, he’d make things difficult with a complaint about Michael’s handling of a 2022 procurement to the State Auditor’s Office. Michael documented that conversation in a confidential HR email to City Administrator Jennifer Bergman on April 19, 2023, and was advised by our labor attorney during a May 2023 conference call to minimize one‑on‑one contact with that vendor.
2. Confirm language for the recommended action motion. One draft reads, “I move that the Council approve this change order even though we don’t yet have a clear funding source identified for the overage, with the understanding that we’ll figure that out during the next budget amendment.”
3. Decide which meeting date to request and update the form before end of day.

metadata.title: Council agenda prep – confirm requested meeting date for Unit #24 item

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Thompson <mthompson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: City Administrator <cadmin@ci.brainerd.mn.us>; City Clerk <cityclerk@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Cc: Dave Larson <dlarson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Date: 2026-02-11 08:42
Subject: Target date for snowplow replacement agenda item

Hi Eric and Julie,

I’m working on the council agenda request for the tandem-axle plow replacement (Unit #24) and want to make sure I’m lining up with your expectations on timing.

Given the volume of material I need to include—photos from the February 5, 2026 blizzard, the maintenance history, and a brief explanation of why I’m requesting this purchase now—I’m aiming to have my full packet draft to you by the end of the day on **Wednesday, March 4, 2026**. If that works, could we slot the item for the **March 23, 2026 council meeting**? That would give us enough time to adjust if finance has concerns.

In the narrative, I plan to explain that during the February 5 blizzard, when I was out with Dave on the night shift, Unit #24 stalled twice on County Road 48 and once on Oak Street near Essentia Health–St. Joseph’s Medical Center. On the second stall around 2:30 a.m., I slipped helping him reconnect a frozen hydraulic line and aggravated the lower back injury I’ve been seeing Dr. Karen Ellingson at Brainerd Orthopedic & Spine Center for since my December 2023 work comp fall in the garage.

I know this issue is coming to you later than it should have because I kept hoping the complaints would die down on their own and didn’t prioritize getting it on the agenda until a few council members started getting calls.

Let me know if March 23 is acceptable or if you’d prefer we push to April 6.

Thanks,
Michael

metadata.subject: Target date for snowplow replacement agenda item

artifacts.content:
Title: Draft agenda language – plow purchase
Date: 2026-03-04 15:00–16:30
Location: Michael’s Office – Public Works
Attendees: Michael Thompson

Description:
Block time to finalize agenda item basics and draft recommended motion for tandem-axle plow purchase before sending packet to Julie.

To cover in write-up:
- Clarify that the recommended action motion is to approve the purchase as outlined so I can avoid a repeat of the January 15, 2025 incident on Highway 210, when Dave Larson and I had to call my cardiologist, Dr. Karen Ellingson at Essentia Health–Brainerd, from the cab of Truck #24 because my blood pressure spiked during a 14‑hour plowing shift and I nearly blacked out while trying to keep the route open with a failing sander.
- Add a short section on alternatives and impacts. To be honest, we didn’t put much time into evaluating other design options because this layout matched what our contractor was already set up to build, but council will still expect at least a paragraph addressing that.
- Note that the target council date I’ve discussed with Eric is **March 23, 2026**, so the requested meeting date on the form should line up with that.
- Double‑check cost escalation language and reference the 2026–2030 CIP.

Reminder: email draft to Eric for a quick sanity check by Friday afternoon so he’s not seeing it for the first time in the packet.

From: Julie Anderson <cityclerk@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: Michael Thompson <mthompson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Date: 2026-02-24 10:19
Subject: RE: Agenda Request – Tandem Plow Replacement

Michael,

Thanks for sending the draft form. I’ve looked over the agenda item basics section and everything is in good shape, but the requested meeting date you wrote in—**April 6, 2026**—doesn’t match what Eric has on his planning calendar.

He’s tentatively slotted the snowplow replacement for the **March 23, 2026 regular council meeting**, assuming you can get the supporting documents to us by March 11. If you’d rather push to April 6 because of the vendor quote timing, that’s fine, but please update the form so the requested meeting date and the narrative are consistent.

Also, in the “Summary of Issue” section, you mention that the situation escalated partly because you told a few residents early on that you’d “figure something out” without first checking whether council would realistically support any of the fixes they were asking for. That level of candor is good, but you might want to soften the wording a bit.

Once you finalize the requested date, send me the revised PDF and I’ll queue it in the agenda system. If you can get that back to me by next Tuesday, we’ll be in good shape.

Julie

Julie Anderson
City Clerk
City of Brainerd

metadata.subject: RE: Agenda Request – Tandem Plow Replacement

artifacts.content:
Title: Council Meeting – Snowplow Replacement Item
Date: 2026-03-23 18:30–21:00
Location: Brainerd City Hall – Council Chambers
Attendees: Mayor; City Council; Michael Thompson; City Administrator; City Clerk

Description:
Regular council meeting where Michael’s agenda item on the tandem-axle plow purchase (Unit #24) is scheduled.

Notes for Michael:
- Be prepared to walk through the requested action motion: approve the purchase so we can retire Truck #18, which was involved in the February 3, 2024 rear‑end collision near Brainerd High School that led to the city’s settlement of Evan Thompson’s classmate’s injury claim for $47,500 after the city’s insurer and the family’s attorney at Crow Wing Legal Aid alleged our older braking system contributed to the crash.
- Emphasize that, starting with the 2026–2027 winter season, the new truck will help reduce Michael’s own overtime plow hours from the 62 hours logged during the December 20–26, 2024 storm—when he missed daughter Natalie’s intake meeting with school counselor Ms. Jensen at Brainerd High School about her anxiety and failing grades—by ensuring a fully reliable tandem‑axle unit for the night crew.
- Clarify that the requested meeting date on the original agenda form was initially entered as **April 6, 2026**, but staff coordinated to move the item up to this March 23 meeting to meet vendor quote deadlines.
